{"stories":{"featured":[{"story_id":192747,"story_date":1779111155,"slug":"death-kneel-remembering-well-review","description":"The Tomb Mold drummer and vocalist taps into a galaxy's worth of musical impulses with this fascinating noise project.","full_text":"Noise can feel like drowning. This is often intentional and a large part of its appeal to the sickos, but even the largest of whales comes up for air every 15 minutes—breaking the surface tension, pupils constricting toward the atmosphere, and taking a bus-sized gulp. Is there anything that experimental noise can learn from this mammalian constraint?

Max Klebanoff is working on it. For several years, his project Death Kneel has kneaded noise music into a wide range of shapes, from diving directly into the harsh void on 2023’s Dawn Simulation, released via très fashion noise imprint Chondritic Sound, to the cavernous, waterlogged ambiance of 2025’s Ink Wash Apparition. On his new release Remembering Well, he picks up those various threads and weaves them together to create an album showcasing the studied breadth that Death Kneel has developed over time. Consider it a counterpoint to his work as the drummer and vocalist of Tomb Mold. Where that band is best known for prog-death epics that operate on a cosmic scale, Death Kneel—and Remembering Well–reflect an ethos that’s more tactile and down-to-earth; an intimate experience as opposed to a monolithic construct.

Where a more typical noise act might confront the listener with a familiar wall of feedback and abrasion, blasting the source into abstraction and disorienting the listener, Klebanoff does something more interesting. He invites us to identify his sources by making them audible: contact mics scraped over furniture, a nasty coughing fit, familiar materials meeting at varying velocities, best exemplified in the song “Going Away.” The listening experience isn’t predicated on some distant, formless roar, but a lifelike perspective that effectively shrinks the listener down to the music’s level; you are the coins in the pocket, you become the crisp packet in the ashtray. In forgoing the disjointed perspectives and decayed soundscapes traditionally associated with noise, and by fostering a link between the sound world and the listener, Klebanoff subtly challenges the genre’s longstanding nihilism, offering a welcome break from tradition.

Abrasion is only one of the album’s strengths. At times, these songs reorient themselves between field recordings, bright, blinding feedback, and rainy ambiance in their adjacent passages, as on “Coax.” Klebanoff reaches into Death Kneel’s filing cabinet and locates the thing he is looking for, examines it, and confidently moves on to his search for the next. The album is obviously a statement in sound and stands up as such, but by building in so much legibility, Klebanoff is also granting us an insight into its creation—a refreshing reframing of the noise artist as tinkerer, exploring the dormant possibilities of materials and teasing narratives from them in a way only human intervention can. Remembering Well then is a reflection of Klebanoff’s curiosity, filtered through his underlying understanding of a galaxy’s worth of musical impulses.

–Luke Jackson","title":"Death Kneel, “Remembering metal per se—it's something else, and that something is fascinating.","full_text":"“Paradise Metal” is a sleight of hand. The music within has very little to do with metal in the conventional sense, and everything to do with the genre’s outer limits—the kind of undulating drone movements practiced by Earth and SUNN O))). The folks at Heat Crimes and their partner label Elhellel will have you know that this music is created by one Father Dionysios Tabakis, an Orthodox priest who serves at the Church of the Panagitsa in Nafplio, Greece. His instruments include the ney, oud, and lyra, among others. The idea is that “Paradise Metal” is a kind of ancient trance music that addresses age-old maladies: depression, alienation, and isolation.

Opener “Relaxation Music with Tanbur” recalls choice moments from Ravi Shankar’s 1967 performance at the Monterey International Pop Festival, mashed up with one of those New Age Environments LPs from the ‘70s—specifically Environments 1, which pairs The Psychologically Ultimate Seashore with Optimum Aviary. You can hear the tide and the birds on “Relaxation…” but unlike those blissed-out field recordings, this wasn’t recorded at Coney Island or the Bronx Aviary to “improve concentration, enhance sleep and/or sex, or impart a sense of calm to the listener.” And yet it may very well do those things. Almost certainly the last one. But it’s a setup. A red herring. A false dawn.

The sense of calm is replaced with one of existential unease, or at least a vaguely ominous fretless guitar on the second track. Both tone and vibe are reminiscent of Neil Young’s indelible score to Jim Jarmusch’s 1995 psychedelic black-and-white Western, Dead Man. Word on the street? This fretless guitar can produce intervals smaller than a semitone, which is why it doesn’t sound like a guitar at all, but more like a sitar. Father T goes fretless again on track five, conjuring a low drone accompanied by devotional chanting.

The psychedelic warbling of track three sets the stage for the spoken word piece that introduces track four, a drunken desert mirage that leads to a liturgical chant (track five) and the kind of lonesome sand drones that Dylan Carlson dreams of when he falls asleep to Ry Cooder’s score for Paris, Texas (track six).

Tracks seven and eight offer techno themes, with distant dance beats produced by artificial drums. If the concept of a “Techno Christmas” appeals to you, well, Father T is your Santa Claus. Part ‘90s Euro disco, part Mediterranean devotional, it’s a collision of the ancient and modern as much as a collusion of religious music and hedonistic club jams.

A singer named Evgenia Symela Armeni appears on the last two tracks. Her hypnotic voice calls through the mist or the ether or the bardo pond, haunting the chapel that is Father T’s house of worship. Like the rest of the album, it transcends genre. Is it metal? Absolutely not. But that was never really the point.

–J. Bennett","title":"π.
